nodes.title: Identify the inclination of a line with the x-axis
nodes.brief: Recognise the inclination as the anticlockwise angle a line makes with the positive x-axis, lying between 0 and 180 degrees.
nodes.quote: The angle (say)  $\theta$  made by the line *l* with positive direction of *x*-axis and measured anti clockwise is called the *inclination of the line*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Measure the angle anticlockwise from the positive x-axis to the line
Place the protractor at the point where the line (or its extension) meets a horizontal reference. Rotate anticlockwise from the positive x-direction until you reach the line; the angle swept is the inclination theta. Example: a line rising to the right that makes a 60-degree sweep anticlockwise has inclination theta = 60 degrees.

PART 2 — Restrict the inclination to the range 0 <= theta <= 180 degrees
Because a line has no preferred direction, any angle beyond 180 degrees duplicates an angle already in [0, 180]. The convention pins the inclination inside this half-turn window, so each non-vertical line has exactly one inclination. Example: a line slanting down to the right has inclination between 90 degrees and 180 degrees, never a negative value.

PART 3 — Assign 0 degrees to a horizontal line and 90 degrees to a vertical line
A horizontal line makes no anticlockwise sweep from the positive x-axis, so theta = 0 degrees. A vertical line makes a quarter-turn anticlockwise, so theta = 90 degrees; this is the only inclination value for which the slope (tan theta) is undefined. Example: the x-axis itself has theta = 0 degrees, and the y-axis has theta = 90 degrees.
keyMoves: Measure the angle anticlockwise from the positive x-axis to the line
Restrict the inclination to the range 0 <= theta <= 180 degrees
Assign 0 degrees to a horizontal line and 90 degrees to a vertical line
misconceptions: Measuring the angle clockwise or from the y-axis instead of anticlockwise from the positive x-axis
Allowing inclinations greater than 180 degrees

nodes.title: Define the slope of a line as tan of its inclination
nodes.brief: Define slope m = tan(theta) from the inclination, and recognise when it is zero or undefined.
nodes.quote: If  $\theta$  is the inclination of a line *l*, then tan  $\theta$  is called the *slope* or *gradient* of the line *l*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the slope as m = tan(theta) from the inclination theta
The slope m captures how steeply a line rises or falls per unit of horizontal movement. It equals the tangent of the inclination: m = tan(theta). Example: a line with inclination 45 degrees has m = tan(45) = 1, meaning it rises one unit for every one unit of horizontal travel.

PART 2 — State that the slope is undefined when theta = 90 degrees (vertical line)
tan(90 degrees) does not exist (the tangent function has a vertical asymptote there), so a vertical line has no numerical slope. Saying the slope is undefined is not a cop-out; it correctly reflects the fact that a vertical line has infinite steepness. Example: the y-axis has inclination 90 degrees and its slope is undefined — never write m = infinity.

PART 3 — Recall the slope of the x-axis is zero and the slope of the y-axis is undefined
The x-axis has inclination 0 degrees so m = tan(0) = 0: it is flat. The y-axis has inclination 90 degrees so its slope is undefined: it is vertical. These are the two boundary cases to memorise before any slope calculation. Example: any line parallel to the x-axis has slope 0; any line parallel to the y-axis has undefined slope.
keyMoves: Compute the slope as m = tan(theta) from the inclination theta
State that the slope is undefined when theta = 90 degrees (vertical line)
Recall the slope of the x-axis is zero and the slope of the y-axis is undefined
misconceptions: Assigning a numerical slope to a vertical line instead of calling it undefined
Confusing the inclination angle itself with the slope (forgetting to take the tangent)

nodes.title: Compute the slope from the coordinates of two points
nodes.brief: Find the slope of a non-vertical line through two given points as the ratio of differences of their coordinates.
nodes.quote: Consequently, we see that in both the cases the slope m of the line through the points
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Apply m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for two points on the line
Given two points P(x1, y1) and Q(x2, y2) on a non-vertical line, the slope is the vertical change divided by the horizontal change: m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1). The order of the two points is arbitrary provided it is consistent in numerator and denominator. Example: for P(1, 2) and Q(4, 6), m = (6 - 2)/(4 - 1) = 4/3.

PART 2 — Identify the rise (difference in y) over the run (difference in x)
The numerator y2 - y1 is the rise (positive if the line goes up, negative if it goes down) and the denominator x2 - x1 is the run (always nonzero for a non-vertical line). Drawing the right triangle between the two points makes the rise and run visible. Example: P(1, 2) to Q(4, 6) gives rise = 4 and run = 3, so the slope triangle has legs 4 and 3.

PART 3 — Recognise the slope is undefined when x1 = x2 (a vertical line)
If both points share the same x-coordinate, the run is zero and the division is undefined. This signals a vertical line, which has no slope. Example: the line through P(2, 5) and Q(2, 1) is vertical (both have x = 2), so its slope is undefined.
keyMoves: Apply m = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for two points on the line
Identify the rise (difference in y) over the run (difference in x)
Recognise the slope is undefined when x1 = x2 (a vertical line)
misconceptions: Writing the difference of x over the difference of y (inverting the ratio)
Being inconsistent about the order of the points in the numerator and denominator

nodes.title: Test two lines for parallelism using their slopes
nodes.brief: Use the rule that two non-vertical lines are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal.
nodes.quote: two non vertical lines  $l_1$  and  $l_2$  are parallel if and only if their slopes are equal.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the slope of each line
Before comparing, find m1 and m2 by rearranging each line to slope-intercept form y = mx + c or by using the two-point formula. Both slopes must be defined (neither line vertical) for this test to apply. Example: from 2x - y + 3 = 0 read m1 = 2; from 4x - 2y + 1 = 0 read m2 = 2.

PART 2 — Conclude the lines are parallel exactly when m1 = m2
Parallel lines make the same angle with the x-axis, so their inclinations are equal and hence tan of those angles (the slopes) are equal. The converse is equally true on [0, 180): equal tangents imply equal angles imply parallel lines. Example: m1 = m2 = 2 confirms the two lines above are parallel.

PART 3 — Remember the test applies to non-vertical lines (both vertical lines are also parallel)
When both lines are vertical their slopes are undefined, yet they are parallel. So the slope test is only stated for non-vertical lines; vertical lines must be handled separately by checking that both have the form x = c. Example: x = 3 and x = 7 are parallel vertical lines even though neither slope is defined.
keyMoves: Compute the slope of each line
Conclude the lines are parallel exactly when m1 = m2
Remember the test applies to non-vertical lines (both vertical lines are also parallel)
misconceptions: Thinking equal slopes only suggest, rather than guarantee, parallelism
Applying the slope test to vertical lines whose slope is undefined

nodes.title: Test two lines for perpendicularity using their slopes
nodes.brief: Use the rule that two non-vertical lines are perpendicular if and only if the product of their slopes is -1.
nodes.quote: two non-vertical lines are perpendicular to each other if and only if their slopes are negative reciprocals of each other,
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the slope of each line
Find m1 and m2 from the given equations or coordinates. Both lines must be non-vertical for the product test to apply; if one is vertical and the other horizontal they are perpendicular by inspection. Example: from y = 2x + 1 read m1 = 2; from y = -x/2 + 3 read m2 = -1/2.

PART 2 — Conclude the lines are perpendicular exactly when m1 m2 = -1
If the inclinations differ by 90 degrees, the tangent-addition identity gives m2 = -1/m1, so m1 m2 = -1. The converse holds too: if the product is -1 then the inclinations differ by 90 degrees and the lines cross at right angles. Example: m1 m2 = 2 * (-1/2) = -1, confirming the lines above are perpendicular.

PART 3 — Equivalently, check that one slope is the negative reciprocal of the other
Flipping the fraction and changing the sign gives the perpendicular slope in one step. If m1 = p/q then the perpendicular slope is m2 = -q/p. Example: the slope perpendicular to 3/4 is -4/3; check: (3/4)(-4/3) = -1.
keyMoves: Compute the slope of each line
Conclude the lines are perpendicular exactly when m1 m2 = -1
Equivalently, check that one slope is the negative reciprocal of the other
misconceptions: Using m1 m2 = 1 (equal product instead of -1) for perpendicularity
Taking the reciprocal without the negative sign

nodes.title: Find the acute angle between two lines from their slopes
nodes.brief: Compute the acute angle between two intersecting non-vertical lines using the tangent formula in their slopes.
nodes.quote: the acute angle (say  $\theta$ ) between lines L<sub>1</sub> and L<sub>2</sub> with slopes  $m_1$  and  $m_2$ , respectively, is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Apply tan(theta) = |(m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2)| with the two slopes
This formula comes from the tangent-subtraction identity applied to the inclinations: tan(alpha2 - alpha1) = (m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2). Taking the absolute value ensures we get the acute angle rather than a signed angle. Example: for m1 = 1 and m2 = 2, tan(theta) = |(2 - 1)/(1 + 2)| = 1/3, so theta = arctan(1/3).

PART 2 — Take the absolute value to obtain the acute angle theta
Without the absolute value the formula can return a negative tangent, corresponding to the obtuse angle between the lines rather than the acute one. The absolute value always picks the angle in (0, 90 degrees]. Example: if m1 = 3 and m2 = -1/3 then the product m1 m2 = -1, denominator = 0, and the lines are perpendicular (theta = 90 degrees, handled as a special case).

PART 3 — Get the obtuse angle as phi = 180 degrees - theta
The two supplementary angles at the intersection are theta and phi = 180 degrees - theta. Once you have the acute angle theta, the obtuse angle is immediate. Example: if theta = 45 degrees then phi = 135 degrees; both are valid angles at the intersection but the formula gives the acute one.
keyMoves: Apply tan(theta) = |(m2 - m1)/(1 + m1 m2)| with the two slopes
Take the absolute value to obtain the acute angle theta
Get the obtuse angle as phi = 180 degrees - theta
misconceptions: Dropping the absolute value and reporting a negative tangent or the wrong (obtuse) angle
Using the formula when 1 + m1 m2 = 0 (perpendicular lines), where it is undefined

nodes.title: Test three points for collinearity using equal slopes
nodes.brief: Decide whether three points lie on one line by checking that the slope of one segment equals the slope of an adjacent segment.
nodes.quote: Three points A, B and C are collinear, if and only if slope of AB = slope of BC.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the slope of AB and the slope of BC from the coordinates
For three points A(x1, y1), B(x2, y2), C(x3, y3), calculate slope of AB = (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) and slope of BC = (y3 - y2)/(x3 - x2). Both must be defined (neither segment vertical in the same direction) for the test to be applied directly. Example: A(1, 1), B(2, 3), C(3, 5): slope AB = (3-1)/(2-1) = 2; slope BC = (5-3)/(3-2) = 2.

PART 2 — Conclude the points are collinear exactly when these two slopes are equal
If slope AB = slope BC then the two segments share point B and have the same direction, so they lie on one straight line. The test is an if-and-only-if. Example: slope AB = slope BC = 2 confirms A, B, C are collinear.

PART 3 — Recognise this is equivalent to the triangle ABC having zero area
The area of triangle ABC using coordinates is (1/2)|x1(y2 - y3) + x2(y3 - y1) + x3(y1 - y2)|; it equals zero exactly when the three points are collinear. Either the slope-equality or the zero-area test can be used. Example: for A(1,1), B(2,3), C(3,5): area = (1/2)|1(3-5) + 2(5-1) + 3(1-3)| = (1/2)|-2 + 8 - 6| = 0, confirming collinearity.
keyMoves: Compute the slope of AB and the slope of BC from the coordinates
Conclude the points are collinear exactly when these two slopes are equal
Recognise this is equivalent to the triangle ABC having zero area
misconceptions: Comparing non-adjacent segments without a shared point and mishandling the common vertex
Concluding collinearity from a near-equality without exact equality of slopes

nodes.title: Write the equations of horizontal and vertical lines
nodes.brief: Give the equation y = a or y = -a for a horizontal line and x = b or x = -b for a vertical line at a fixed distance from an axis.
nodes.quote: If a horizontal line L is at a distance *a* from the *x*-axis then ordinate of every point lying on the line is either *a* or -a
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write a horizontal line as y = constant (y = a or y = -a)
Every point on a horizontal line has the same y-coordinate (ordinate), regardless of its x-coordinate. So the equation is simply y = a (if the line is a units above the x-axis) or y = -a (if it is a units below). Example: the line 3 units above the x-axis is y = 3; two units below is y = -2.

PART 2 — Write a vertical line as x = constant (x = b or x = -b)
Every point on a vertical line has the same x-coordinate (abscissa). So the equation is x = b (b units to the right of the y-axis) or x = -b (b units to the left). Example: the line 4 units to the right of the y-axis is x = 4; two units to the left is x = -2.

PART 3 — Choose the sign from whether the line is above/below or left/right of the axis
The sign in the equation directly encodes which side of the axis the line lies on. A positive constant means the positive half of that coordinate; a negative constant means the negative half. Example: a horizontal line through the point (-5, 3) has equation y = 3 (positive, above x-axis); a vertical line through (-5, 3) has equation x = -5 (negative, left of y-axis).
keyMoves: Write a horizontal line as y = constant (y = a or y = -a)
Write a vertical line as x = constant (x = b or x = -b)
Choose the sign from whether the line is above/below or left/right of the axis
misconceptions: Swapping the forms: writing x = a for a horizontal line or y = b for a vertical line
Picking the wrong sign for the constant relative to the axis

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in point-slope form
nodes.brief: Derive and apply y - y0 = m(x - x0) for a line of slope m through a fixed point (x0, y0).
nodes.quote: the point (x, y) lies on the line with slope *m* through the fixed point  $(x_0, y_0)$ , if and only if, its coordinates satisfy the equation
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the known point (x0, y0) and the slope m
The point-slope form needs exactly one point on the line and the slope. Read both from the problem statement before writing any equation. Example: 'a line through (-2, 3) with slope -4' gives (x0, y0) = (-2, 3) and m = -4.

PART 2 — Substitute into y - y0 = m(x - x0)
By the slope definition, (y - y0)/(x - x0) = m for any other point (x, y) on the line; rearranging gives the point-slope form. Substitute the values directly without rearranging prematurely. Example: y - 3 = -4(x - (-2)), which is y - 3 = -4(x + 2).

PART 3 — Simplify to the form Ax + By + C = 0 if required
Expand and collect all terms on one side. Keep coefficients as integers whenever possible by clearing any fractions. Example: y - 3 = -4(x + 2) expands to y - 3 = -4x - 8, giving 4x + y + 5 = 0.
keyMoves: Identify the known point (x0, y0) and the slope m
Substitute into y - y0 = m(x - x0)
Simplify to the form Ax + By + C = 0 if required
misconceptions: Writing y - x0 = m(x - y0), mixing up coordinates and the point
Dropping the subtraction and writing y = m(x - x0) when y0 is non-zero

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in two-point form
nodes.brief: Derive and apply the equation of a line through two given points using the slope between them.
nodes.quote: Thus, equation of the line passing through the points  $(x_1, y_1)$  and  $(x_2, y_2)$  is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the slope (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) from the two points
With two distinct points P1(x1, y1) and P2(x2, y2) the slope is determined uniquely (provided x1 is not equal to x2). Compute this first. Example: P1(1, -1) and P2(3, 5) give slope = (5 - (-1))/(3 - 1) = 6/2 = 3.

PART 2 — Substitute into y - y1 = ((y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1))(x - x1)
This is just point-slope form with the slope filled in. Use either point as the base; the equation is the same either way. Example: y - (-1) = 3(x - 1), i.e. y + 1 = 3(x - 1).

PART 3 — Clear the fraction and simplify to a linear equation
Multiply through by (x2 - x1) to eliminate the fraction, then expand and collect terms. Example: y + 1 = 3(x - 1) => y + 1 = 3x - 3 => 3x - y - 4 = 0.
keyMoves: Compute the slope (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) from the two points
Substitute into y - y1 = ((y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1))(x - x1)
Clear the fraction and simplify to a linear equation
misconceptions: Mismatching which point supplies (x1, y1) in the slope versus the (x - x1) factor
Inverting the slope ratio while writing the two-point form

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in slope-intercept form
nodes.brief: Use y = mx + c for a line with slope m and y-intercept c, and the variant y = m(x - d) for a given x-intercept.
nodes.quote: Thus, the point (x, y) on the line with slope *m* and *y*-intercept *c* lies on the line if and only if
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Use y = mx + c when the slope m and y-intercept c are known
A line of slope m crossing the y-axis at (0, c) passes through (0, c), so point-slope form gives y - c = m(x - 0), hence y = mx + c. The constant c is the signed y-intercept. Example: slope 3 and y-intercept -2 give y = 3x - 2.

PART 2 — Use y = m(x - d) when the slope m and x-intercept d are known
If the line crosses the x-axis at (d, 0), then point-slope form at that point gives y - 0 = m(x - d), i.e. y = m(x - d). Example: slope 1/2 and x-intercept 4 give y = (1/2)(x - 4), i.e. x - 2y - 4 = 0.

PART 3 — Read off slope and intercept by rearranging a given equation into y = mx + c
Isolate y on the left side; the coefficient of x is the slope and the constant term is the y-intercept. Example: 3x - 4y + 8 = 0 => 4y = 3x + 8 => y = (3/4)x + 2; slope = 3/4, y-intercept = 2.
keyMoves: Use y = mx + c when the slope m and y-intercept c are known
Use y = m(x - d) when the slope m and x-intercept d are known
Read off slope and intercept by rearranging a given equation into y = mx + c
misconceptions: Treating c as the x-intercept rather than the y-intercept
Forgetting the sign of c when the line cuts the negative y-axis

nodes.title: Write a line's equation in intercept form
nodes.brief: Use x/a + y/b = 1 for a line with x-intercept a and y-intercept b on the axes.
nodes.quote: Thus, equation of the line making intercepts a and b on x-and y-axis, respectively, is
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the x-intercept a and the y-intercept b
The x-intercept a is where the line crosses the x-axis (the point (a, 0)) and the y-intercept b is where it crosses the y-axis (the point (0, b)). Both must be non-zero for the intercept form to apply; a line through the origin cannot be written in this form. Example: a line through (3, 0) and (0, 2) has a = 3 and b = 2.

PART 2 — Substitute into x/a + y/b = 1
The two-point form applied to (a, 0) and (0, b) simplifies to exactly x/a + y/b = 1. Substitute the values of a and b directly. Example: a = 3, b = 2 gives x/3 + y/2 = 1.

PART 3 — Clear denominators to get a linear equation
Multiply through by the LCM of a and b to obtain an integer-coefficient form. Example: x/3 + y/2 = 1, multiply by 6: 2x + 3y = 6, i.e. 2x + 3y - 6 = 0.
keyMoves: Identify the x-intercept a and the y-intercept b
Substitute into x/a + y/b = 1
Clear denominators to get a linear equation
misconceptions: Writing x/b + y/a = 1, swapping the two intercepts
Setting the right-hand side to 0 instead of 1

nodes.title: Recognise the general equation of a line Ax + By + C = 0
nodes.brief: Recognise that every line is Ax + By + C = 0 with A, B not both zero, and convert between this and the other forms.
nodes.quote: Any equation of the form Ax + By + C = 0, where A and B are not zero simultaneously is called general linear equation or general equation of a line.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Rearrange any line equation into Ax + By + C = 0
Collect all terms to the left side and clear fractions so that A, B, C are integers (preferably with no common factor). Every one of the specific forms (point-slope, slope-intercept, intercept, two-point) reduces to this standard form. Example: y = (3/4)x + 2 becomes 3x - 4y + 8 = 0 after multiplying through by 4.

PART 2 — Read the slope as -A/B and y-intercept as -C/B (when B is not zero)
Rearrange Ax + By + C = 0 to y = (-A/B)x + (-C/B): coefficient of x is the slope and the constant is the y-intercept. When B = 0 the line is vertical and has no slope or y-intercept. Example: for 3x - 4y + 8 = 0, slope = -3/(-4) = 3/4 and y-intercept = -8/(-4) = 2.

PART 3 — Reduce a general equation to slope-intercept or intercept form to find slopes and intercepts
Divide through by -C to obtain the intercept form x/(-C/A) + y/(-C/B) = 1 (when C is not zero), giving x-intercept -C/A and y-intercept -C/B. Example: 3x - 4y + 8 = 0, divide by -8: -3x/8 + 4y/8 = 1 => x/(-8/3) + y/2 = 1; x-intercept = -8/3, y-intercept = 2.
keyMoves: Rearrange any line equation into Ax + By + C = 0
Read the slope as -A/B and y-intercept as -C/B (when B is not zero)
Reduce a general equation to slope-intercept or intercept form to find slopes and intercepts
misconceptions: Allowing both A and B to be zero (which gives no line)
Misreading the slope as A/B instead of -A/B

nodes.title: Compute the perpendicular distance of a point from a line
nodes.brief: Apply d = |A x1 + B y1 + C| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) for the distance of a point from a line in general form.
nodes.quote: Thus, the perpendicular distance (d) of a line Ax + By + C = 0 from a point  $(x_1, y_1)$  is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Put the line in general form Ax + By + C = 0 and read off A, B, C
The distance formula requires the line in standard form. Rearrange the given equation so all terms are on the left with the right side equal to zero, and identify the three coefficients A, B, C. Example: for 3x - 4y - 26 = 0, read A = 3, B = -4, C = -26.

PART 2 — Substitute the point into A x1 + B y1 + C and take the absolute value
Plug the coordinates of the given point into the expression Ax1 + By1 + C and compute its numerical value, then wrap it in absolute value bars. The sign of Ax1 + By1 + C indicates which side of the line the point is on, but distance is always positive. Example: point (3, -5): 3(3) + (-4)(-5) + (-26) = 9 + 20 - 26 = 3; |3| = 3.

PART 3 — Divide by sqrt(A^2 + B^2) to get the distance d
The denominator is the magnitude of the normal vector (A, B) and it normalises the expression to a true length. Example: sqrt(3^2 + (-4)^2) = sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5; d = 3/5.
keyMoves: Put the line in general form Ax + By + C = 0 and read off A, B, C
Substitute the point into A x1 + B y1 + C and take the absolute value
Divide by sqrt(A^2 + B^2) to get the distance d
misconceptions: Forgetting the absolute value, which can give a negative distance
Dividing by A^2 + B^2 instead of its square root, or applying the formula without first putting the line in general form

nodes.title: Compute the distance between two parallel lines
nodes.brief: Apply d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) for two parallel lines written with the same A and B.
nodes.quote: Thus, the distance d between two parallel lines  $y = mx + c_1$  and  $y = mx + c_2$  is given by
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write both parallel lines with the same A and B coefficients
Parallel lines have equal slopes, so their general-form equations differ only in the constant term. If necessary, scale one equation so the A and B coefficients match. Example: 3x - 4y + 7 = 0 and 3x - 4y + 5 = 0 already share A = 3, B = -4.

PART 2 — Apply d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) (or |c1 - c2| / sqrt(1 + m^2) in slope form)
This formula comes from picking any point on one line and computing its distance to the other line using the point-to-line formula. The A and B coefficients must be identical before applying it. Example: d = |7 - 5| / sqrt(9 + 16) = 2/5.

PART 3 — Take the absolute difference of the constant terms
Only the C values differ between the two equations; the formula needs their absolute difference. Forgetting the absolute value can yield a negative distance if C2 > C1. Example: |7 - 5| = 2, so d = 2/sqrt(25) = 2/5.
keyMoves: Write both parallel lines with the same A and B coefficients
Apply d = |C1 - C2| / sqrt(A^2 + B^2) (or |c1 - c2| / sqrt(1 + m^2) in slope form)
Take the absolute difference of the constant terms
misconceptions: Using the formula before making the coefficients of x and y identical in both equations
Adding C1 and C2 instead of taking their difference

nodes.title: Represent a straight line algebraically and solve line problems
nodes.brief: Combine slope, the various forms of a line's equation, and the distance formulas to represent any straight line algebraically and solve problems about lines.
nodes.quote: Main focus is on representing the line algebraically, for which *slope* is most essential.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Choose the equation form (point-slope, two-point, slope-intercept, intercept, or general) that matches the given data
Each form is best suited to a specific type of given information. Point-slope is fastest when a point and slope are known; two-point when two points are given; intercept-form when both axis intercepts are given; slope-intercept when slope and y-intercept are given; general form as the universal fallback. Example: given 'line through (2, 3) with slope 5', choose point-slope: y - 3 = 5(x - 2).

PART 2 — Use slopes to handle parallelism, perpendicularity, angle, and collinearity conditions
All four geometric relationships between lines reduce to slope algebra: equal slopes for parallelism; product -1 for perpendicularity; the tangent formula for the angle; and equal adjacent slopes for collinearity. Example: to find a line through (1, 1) parallel to 2x - 3y + 5 = 0 (slope 2/3), write y - 1 = (2/3)(x - 1), giving 2x - 3y + 1 = 0.

PART 3 — Apply the perpendicular-distance formulas to compute point-to-line and parallel-line distances
The two distance formulas (d = |Ax1 + By1 + C|/sqrt(A^2 + B^2) and d = |C1 - C2|/sqrt(A^2 + B^2)) require the line(s) in general form with matching coefficients. Example: distance of (-2, 3) from 12x - 5y + 1 = 0 is |12(-2) - 5(3) + 1|/sqrt(144 + 25) = |-38|/13 = 38/13.
keyMoves: Choose the equation form (point-slope, two-point, slope-intercept, intercept, or general) that matches the given data
Use slopes to handle parallelism, perpendicularity, angle, and collinearity conditions
Apply the perpendicular-distance formulas to compute point-to-line and parallel-line distances
misconceptions: Reaching for one fixed form regardless of what data is given, instead of choosing the convenient form
Treating slope, equation forms and distance as unrelated tricks rather than one connected toolkit
